commits.message:
gauge: trim CSS comment to why-only

The block enumerated the gauge's parts (the dial, the proportion scale, the
needle, the LCD) — what the CSS already encodes. [LAW:comments-explain-why-only]
Keep only the rationale: why it's a dial (the movement's no-duplication rule)
and why the glow is a @keyframes not a JS branch (so the one global
prefers-reduced-motion kill governs it).

gauge: derive runway px live from the viewport, not a baked constant

The gauge fabricated a constant 1200px scale for runway length, while the
sibling runway painter in the same movement deliberately measures its px
readout live from the real viewport height ('honest, not a constant'). Two
representations of one concept that could silently diverge whenever the
viewport changed. [LAW:one-source-of-truth]

This page has no resize/rebuild — build() runs once; the honest painters read
live dimensions in paint() every frame. So the fix splits the two
representations by where each can be truthful: the baked dial face becomes a
constant 0-100 proportion scale (exactly what the needle position geometrically
is — never stale, never a fabricated px claim), and the single px figure moves
to the LCD, computed live in paint() from the viewport (a runway runs ~1.5
viewport-heights). 'Runway length in px' now has one representation, with no
second scale to drift from the real track.

Verified in Chrome DevTools: at innerHeight 810 the LCD reads 1220px at full
sweep; resized to 600 it reads 900px — genuinely live. Face labels 0/20/.../100,
clean sweep, zero console errors from the page.

mit-styling-aj1.5.3: the gauge — a graduated runway-length instrument

Restyle the gauge node of the Pin movement from a plain semicircular dial
into a precision measurement instrument: a 240° swept face with major+minor
tick graduations, a numbered px scale (0–1200), a glowing accent fill arc, a
balanced needle (counterweight tail + pulsing glow tip + hub cap), and a
centre LCD readout in the dial's odometer window ticking the live runway
length. One scale spec (MAX, SEG) in build() drives the ticks, the numeric
labels, and the LCD range, so the analog scale and digital readout cannot
disagree. Tick/label geometry derives from one polar() helper.

Stays a DIAL — distinct from the runway scroll-window and terminal editor.
paint(p,n) writes state only; needle + LCD track raw p, caption tracks n.w.
The tip-glow pulse is a CSS @keyframes auto-gated by the global
prefers-reduced-motion kill — no JS phase branch.

Verified in Chrome DevTools: zero console errors, clean sweep across the full
scroll range, needle lands precisely on the 0 and 1200 ticks.

mit-styling-aj1.5.2: the terminal — a syntax-lit code editor that types the rule (#20)

* mit-styling-aj1.5.2: the terminal — a syntax-lit code editor that types the rule

The Pin movement's terminal node was a generic flat dark window: gray dots, a
blue prompt glyph per line, one ink color, a big void of black below. Replace it
with the movement's authoring surface — a real code EDITOR, distinct from the
runway's scroll-window and the gauge's dial:

- Tabbed window chrome (true macOS traffic-light hues) titled sticky.css, the
  .css extension wearing the section's azure.
- A line-number gutter that shares one CSS grid with the code, so numbers can
  never drift from their lines; the current line lights azure + tinted.
- Real syntax highlighting: azure properties, mint values, amber numbers, slate
  italic comments. The source is tokenised ONCE at build into {t,c} segments
  (you can't slice highlighted HTML mid-tag); the per-frame reveal walks those
  segments and block-comment state carries across lines, so the two-line CSS
  comment lights as one span. [LAW:one-source-of-truth]
- Char-by-char typing driven by n.w (round(progress*totalChars)) instead of
  line-by-line jumps, so the scrub uses the full resolution of paint(p,n).
- A status bar that ticks live Ln/Col and flips typing…→saved at completion.
- Window sized to its content and centred — a deliberate object, not a void.

paint(p,n) writes state only (innerHTML/textContent/class); the caret blink is a
CSS @keyframes gated by the global prefers-reduced-motion kill, not a JS branch.
The whole look lives in the terminal painter + its CSS block selected by kind;
the shared paint loop is untouched. [LAW:locality-or-seam] [LAW:decomposition]

Verified in Chrome across the scroll range: zero console errors/warnings,
clean char-by-char scrub from empty through fully-typed/saved.

* terminal tokenizer: route same-line comment suffix through the parser

The same-line /* ... */ branch pushed the comment span but dropped the
suffix after the close, so a declaration trailing a same-line block comment
never flowed through tmDecl. Mirror the multi-line-close branch (which
already tokenizes the suffix) so every line routes through one parser path
and no input is lost. [LAW:dataflow-not-control-flow]

Current data has no such line, but the tokenizer is a general representation
and must be honest about the whole grammar.
